8.1 Enzymes as Biological Catalysts

What is a Catalyst?
A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate or velocity of a chemical reaction without itself being changed in the overall process
A catalyst accelerates the approach to equilibrium for a given reaction without changing the thermodynamic favorability of the reaction
A catalyst lowers the energy barrier (activation energy of the transition state) in the process of converting a substrate into a product

Transition State and Reaction Rate (2 of 5)
However, to determine the rate of a chemical reaction, not only its initial state and its final state have to be considered, but also its transition state
The transition state is an additional energy barrier to convert reactants into products (and vice versa)
The transition state has more free energy in comparison to that of the reactant or product; thus, it is the least stable state during the progress of a chemical reaction

Transition State and Reaction Rate (3 of 5)
Figure 8.2 Free energy diagrams for the simple reaction

Transition State and Reaction Rate (4 of 5)
The rate constant (and hence the overall rate) of a chemical reaction can be increased by raising the temperature of the reaction or by lowering the free energy

Mechanisms for Achieving Rate Acceleration (1 of 2)
On the left, reaction coordinate diagram of a reaction following the equation above. Noncatalyzed reaction in blue, catalyzed reaction in red
Figure 8.10 Reaction coordinate diagram for a simple enzyme-catalyzed reaction.

Mechanisms for Achieving Rate Acceleration (2 of 2)
Enzymatic catalysis proceeds by one or more of:
general acid/base catalysis (G A B C)
covalent catalysis
electrostatic stabilization
proximity effects
preferential stabilization of the transition state
protein conformational changes may also play an important role in catalysis

8.5 Coenzymes, Vitamins, and Essential Metals

Coenzyme or Cofactor Function in Catalysis (1 of 2)
For efficient catalytic activity, enzymes often need  the help of ions or small molecules, which are bound to enzymes, but  not irreversibly changed during catalysis (these ions or molecules are called coenzymes or cofactors)

Multisubstrate Reactions (1 of 4)
Many enzymes catalyze chemical reactions that convert more than one substrate (reactant) into more than one product
For such reactions, the order of the binding and releasing steps becomes an important feature of the enzymatic mechanism
There are at least three types of reactions:
Random substrate binding
Ordered substrate binding
Ping-pong (double displacement) mechanism

Multisubstrate Reactions (2 of 4)
Random substrate binding
Either one of the substrates can be bound first for a reaction to occur (Example: binding of A T P and glucose by hexokinase in the glycolytic pathway)

Multisubstrate Reactions (3 of 4)
Ordered substrate binding
One substrate must bind first before the other(s) can bind significantly. A reaction only occurs when substrates are bound in this order (Examples: dehydrogenase reactions where it is
necessary that the cofactor, i.e.,
or N A D H, binds first)

Multisubstrate Reactions (4 of 4)
Ping-pong (double displacement) mechanism
One substrate is bound and reacts, releasing the first product. Then, a second substrate is bound and reacts, releasing a second product (Examples: serine proteases such as trypsin or chymotrypsin)
8.7 Enzyme Inhibition

Drugs, Toxins, and Enzymatic Activity (1 of 2)
Many prescription drugs are enzyme inhibitors
The development of some drugs has been advanced by kinetic studies, as well as structural studies of the target enzymes
Also, the effects of some natural and synthetic toxins are the result of enzyme inhibition or inactivation
There are two classes of enzyme inhibitors:
reversible inhibitors (noncovalently bound)
irreversible inhibitors (covalently bound)

Drugs, Toxins, and Enzymatic Activity (2 of 2)
Reversible inhibition
While in a reversible inhibition the inhibitor interacts with an enzyme noncovalently, in an irreversible reaction the inhibitor is bound covalently
There are at least three different mode of reversible inhibition:
competitive inhibition
uncompetitive inhibition
mixed or noncompetitive inhibition

Reversible Inhibition (1 of 3)
Competitive inhibition
Figure 8.24 Competitive inhibition.
Both, substrate and inhibitor, can fit the enzyme’s active site. The substrate can be processed, but not the inhibitor

Uncompetitive Inhibition (2 of 3)
Bisubstrate reaction with ordered substrate binding involving an inhibitor that is uncompetitive with respect to substrate S1 and competitive with respect to substrate S2 (Example: inhibition of dihydrofolate reductase by the anticancer drug methotrexate; uncompetitive for N A D P H binding and competitive for dihydrofolate binding

Irreversible Inhibition
Covalent bonding to an enzyme’s active site
Example: diisopropyl fluorophosphate (D F P) binds to the active site serine of acetylcholinesterase, an enzyme involved in nerve conduction. Inhibition of this enzyme causes rapid paralysis
Figure 8.32 Irreversible inhibition by adduct formation.
8.8 The Regulation of Enzyme Activity

Controlling Enzyme Functions in the Cellular Context (1 of 4)
There are various ways to control and regulate the activities of enzymes within cells. Some important example are:
Substrate level control
reaction rate increases with increasing [S]
in general, a large change in [S] would be required to change the activity of an enzyme significantly (thus, this is a crude means of regulation)

Controlling Enzyme Functions in the Cellular Context (2 of 4)
Regulation at committed steps of pathways
feedback inhibition or activation at control points
often mediated by allosteric enzymes
efficient means to maintain homeostasis
Covalent modification
reversible (e.g., many cell signaling processes)
irreversible (e.g., zymogen activation, blood clotting)

Controlling Enzyme Functions in the Cellular Context (3 of 4)
Feedback inhibition

Controlling Enzyme Functions in the Cellular Context (4 of 4)
Allostery
Allosteric enzymes are usually multisubunit proteins that change their conformations upon binding of substrates or effector molecules
In homoallostery, enzymes exhibit cooperativity in substrate binding
In heteroallostery, enzymes are regulated by (nonsubstrate) effector molecules

Allostery (1 of 2)
Homoallostery
Effect of cooperative substrate binding on enzyme kinetics
Figure 8.35 Effect of cooperative substrate binding on enzyme kinetics.

Allostery (2 of 2)
Heteroallostery
Control of a heteroallosteric enzyme by effector molecules (activators or inhibitors)
Figure 8.36 Heteroallosteric control of an enzyme.
8.9 Covalent Modifications Used to Regulate Enzyme Activity

Reversible and Irreversible Modifications (1 of 3)
Many enzymes are covalently modified to activate or deactivate them
in most cases, covalent modification serve as functional on or off switches
Such modifications can be reversible or irreversible:
phosphorylation (carried out by protein kinases) can be reversed by dephosphorylation (performed by protein phosphatases)
zymogens are converted to active enzymes by (irreversible) proteolytic cleavage

Reversible and Irreversible Modifications (2 of 3)
Reversible phosphorylation and dephosphorylation by kinases and phosphatases, respectively
Figure 8.41 Reversible covalent modification by kinases/phosphatases.

Reversible and Irreversible Modifications (3 of 3)
Irreversible zymogen activation by proteolytic cleavage
Figure 8.42 Zymogen activation by proteolytic cleavage.

Irreversible Zymogen Activation by Proteolytic Cleavage
Proteolytic activation of the zymogen chymotrypsinogen to π-chymotrypsin
Figure 8.43 Activation of chymotrypsinogen.
8.10 Nonprotein Biocatalysts : Catalytic Nucleic Acids

Ribonucleic Acids as Enzymes
Ribonucleic acids can catalyze chemical reactions
This type of ribonucleic acids (R N A molecules) are called ribozymes
Many biochemists believe that ancient cells had already R N A-based self-replication and catalytic mechanisms (“R N A World”) before D N A evolved as the main molecule of genetic information and protein became the main molecule of catalytic activity in the cell
8.11 Tools of Biochemistry

Analysis in the Steady State (1 of 6)
Most enzymatic reactions can be measured under steady-state conditions
–  The reaction rate (ν), the
the Michaelis constant
and the turnover number
for a reaction can
be determined when the Michaelis–Menten equation applies
Figure 8.20 The steady state in enzyme kinetics.

Analysis in the Steady State (2 of 6)
Measurements under steady-state conditions (range of seconds to hours) can be carried out using:
Spectrophotometry: substrate or product must absorb light and absorbance must change during the progress of the reaction (substrate and product must absorb light differently)
Fluorescence: similar to spectrophotometry, distinct emission spectra of substrate and product are necessary

Analysis in the Steady State (3 of 6)
Radioactivity assay: substrate is labeled with a radioactive isotope and changes during the conversion to a product are measured. However, separation of isotope in the (initial) substrate state and the (final) product state is necessary
Reactions that are extremely fast require specific techniques, such as:
Stopped flow: Enzyme and substrate are measured while mixed in a flow rate-controlled device

Analysis in the Steady State (4 of 6)
A stopped-flow device
Figure 8A.2 Typical stopped-flow apparatus.

Analysis in the Steady State (5 of 6)
The temperature jump method
Temperature jump: A reaction mixture is at equilibrium at temperature T1. When the temperature is “jumped” to T2, the equilibrium of the reaction will shift, which can be measured
Figure 8A.3 The temperature jump method.

Analysis in the Steady State (6 of 6)
Summary of time scales for kinetic techniques
Figure 8A.1 Time scales for kinetic techniques described here.

Chapter 8 Summary (1 of 3)
An enzyme is a biological catalyst that enhances the rate of a chemical reaction by lowering its activation energy without affecting its equilibrium
Most enzymes in living systems are proteins, some are R N A
An enzyme binds at least one substrate at its active site, forming an enzyme-substrate complex that converts the substrate into a product, which is then released
The enzyme brings the substrate close to the reaction’s transition state (induced fit hypothesis)

Chapter 8 Summary (2 of 3)
Many enzymes require coenzymes or metal ions for their diverse functions
Most simple enzymatic reactions can be described by the Michaelis–Menten equation:
Enzymes can be inhibited reversibly (competitive, uncompetitive, or mixed/noncompetitive) or irreversibly (covalent binding of an inhibiting molecule to the enzyme’s active site)

Chapter 8 Summary (3 of 3)
Activities of enzymes can be regulated in various ways by: e.g., substrate level control, feedback inhibition/activation, or reversible/irreversible covalent protein modification
An example of reversible modification is the phosphorylation and dephosphorylation of enzymes by kinases and phosphatases, respectively, while the proteolytic activation of zymogens is an example of irreversible modification
